behaviors.description: SUM function evaluates to the correct sum of values in a cell range
behaviors.justification: Pure formula evaluation logic with no I/O or external dependencies. The failure is in the SUM algorithm itself (range parsing, accumulation logic). Can be tested by creating cells with known values and verifying the computed result.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells A1 through A10 with known numeric values (1, 2, 3, ..., 10). Create a cell with =SUM(A1:A10). Evaluate and verify result equals 55. Test edge cases: empty cells within range, negative numbers, single cell range, entire column reference.

behaviors.description: Cascaded formula references resolve and propagate values correctly through the dependency chain
behaviors.justification: Tests whether the formula evaluator correctly resolves transitive dependencies in a single evaluation pass. The failure is in dependency resolution (does it evaluate B1 before using its value in C1?). No state management or multi-step triggers required.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1=5. Set B1==A1*2 (formula). Set C1==B1+1 (formula). Evaluate C1 and verify result is 11 (i.e., (5*2)+1). Change A1 to different values and verify C1 produces correct cascaded results. The test passes if C1's evaluation correctly triggers B1's evaluation, which triggers A1's evaluation.

behaviors.description: Circular cell references are detected and return an error instead of infinite loop
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is a graph algorithm that either works or doesn't. No I/O, multi-cell state management, or external systems involved. The failure is in the cycle-detection logic (DFS, memoization, or visited-set tracking).
behaviors.test_description: Set A1==B1 and B1==A1 (simple circular reference). Attempt to evaluate A1. Verify engine returns CIRCULAR_REFERENCE error within reasonable time (not hanging or crashing). Test with longer cycles (A→B→C→A). Test with self-reference (A1==A1).

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP returns correct value for exact match and error when key not found
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP is a built-in function with pure lookup logic. No I/O or external dependencies. The failure is in the search algorithm (binary search, linear search, error handling for missing keys).
behaviors.test_description: Create lookup table in A1:B5 with keys in column A (e.g., 'apple', 'banana', 'cherry') and values in column B. Create cells with =VLOOKUP('banana', A:B, 2, 0) (verify correct value returned). Test with key not in table (verify #N/A error). Test with duplicate keys (verify first match). Test with case sensitivity if applicable.

behaviors.description: Nested IF conditions evaluate correct branch based on condition results
behaviors.justification: IF is a control-flow function. The failure is in condition evaluation or branch selection logic. Pure logic, no I/O or state management.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells with test values: A1=5, A1=15, A1=25. For each, evaluate ==IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, 'high', 'medium'), 'low'). Verify results are 'low', 'medium', 'high' respectively. Test boundary conditions (A1=10, A1=20). Verify nested IF correctly recurses and doesn't short-circuit incorrectly.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins values with separator and skips empty cells without inserting blanks
behaviors.justification: Pure string manipulation logic. No I/O or external dependencies. The failure is in the concatenation algorithm (how empty cells are handled, separator application).
behaviors.test_description: Create cells: A1='hello', A2='' (empty), A3='world'. Create formula ==CONCATENATE(A1, A2, A3) or ==CONCATENATE(A1:A3). Verify result is 'helloworld' (not 'hello||world' or with extra separators). Test with multiple consecutive empty cells, numeric values, and special characters. Verify empty cells contribute nothing to output.

behaviors.description: Formula references to cells in different sheets resolve correctly to the referenced value
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet reference resolution is in-memory logic (reference parsing and sheet lookup). Multiple sheets can coexist in memory without I/O. The failure is in reference parser or sheet lookup (does it parse 'Sheet2!A1' correctly? Does it find Sheet2?).
behaviors.test_description: Create Sheet2 with A1=5. In Sheet1, create cell with ==Sheet2!A1+1. Evaluate and verify result is 6. Test reference to non-existent sheet (verify error). Test various cell addresses (B10, Z100). Verify reference syntax is correctly parsed and sheet is correctly located.

behaviors.description: Changing a cell value triggers recalculation of all dependent formulas in correct topological order
behaviors.justification: Tests the dependency-tracking and recalculation mechanism across multiple cells. A unit test of single evaluation wouldn't catch ordering bugs. The failure is at the state-change-propagation level: when A1 changes, B1 must recalculate before C1, and C1 must use B1's NEW value, not stale value.
behaviors.test_description: Create dependency chain: A1=5, B1==A1*2 (10), C1==B1+1 (11), D1==C1*2 (22). Change A1 to 10. Verify all cells recalculate to B1=20, C1=21, D1=42. Use internal inspection/logging to verify B1 recalculated before C1, C1 before D1 (topological order). Test changes at different points in the chain. Verify no cell uses stale upstream values.

behaviors.description: Undo operation restores both formula text and all dependent cell values after formula edit
behaviors.justification: Undo requires managing snapshots of multiple cells (edited cell plus all dependents). A unit test of formula evaluation wouldn't verify whether dependent cells' saved state is properly restored. The failure is at the state-capture-and-restoration level.
behaviors.test_description: Set A1=5, B1==A1*2 (evaluates to 10). Edit B1 to ==A1*3. Verify B1 now evaluates to 15. Call undo(). Verify B1 contains original formula ==A1*2 and still evaluates to 10. Test with longer undo chains (multiple edits). Test with multiple dependent cells (B1 and C1 both depend on A1; edit A1; verify undo restores both).

behaviors.description: Saving and loading a workbook preserves all cell values, formulas, and floating-point precision with no data loss
behaviors.justification: Real filesystem I/O is required. Mocking file operations would hide serialization bugs, endianness issues, precision loss, and binary format errors. The failure boundary is at the OS level: did the bytes actually round-trip correctly?
behaviors.test_description: Create workbook with: integer cells, floating-point cells (including precision edge cases like 1.23456789), formula cells (=SUM, =VLOOKUP, =IF, cross-sheet refs), empty cells. Save to disk using engine's save(). Load using engine's load(). Verify: all cell values match exactly (including float bit patterns), all formulas are preserved as text, re-evaluating formulas produces identical results. Test floating-point edge cases (very large/small numbers, values near precision boundaries) to catch precision loss.
